definition: Spiritual torpor; the inability to feel; the deadening of moral and emotional responsiveness.
lost: Named the specific sin of not caring — acedia is distinct from sadness because sadness still feels, and from laziness because laziness still wants.
usage: It was not grief that had taken him from his work but something older and more resistant: a pure acedia.

definition: Scorched, dried out; in humoral medicine, a condition in which the blood has been burnt by an internal heat, producing melancholy, brittleness, a characteristic aridity of spirit.
lost: Nothing in modern English names the specific exhaustion that comes from burning from the inside — the feeling of having been dried out by your own heat.
usage: She was adust in a way that sleep no longer touched.

definition: Eternally bright, unfading — of a color or quality that does not diminish with time; named for the amaranth flower, which was believed to be immortal.
lost: "Eternal" describes duration. Amaranthine describes condition — the refusal to fade is active, ongoing.
usage: The grief was amaranthine; it did not soften with the years.

definition: A terse, pointed, memorable saying; a maxim that contains more than its word count suggests.
lost: Named the maxim with the implication that it cuts — an apophthegm is a saying you remember because it drew blood.
usage: He was known for his apophthegms; people collected them the way others collect wounds.

definition: The specific warmth of winter sunlight — warmth that touches the skin without persuading the season, that promises nothing and yet is welcome for exactly what it is.
lost: "Winter sunshine" is meteorological. Apricity names the particular quality: warmth without conviction.
usage: She turned her face to the apricity and did not open her eyes.

definition: The reading of omens from natural signs — birds, weather, chance events — and by extension, any portent or sign; the practice of treating the world as legible, as actively communicating.
lost: "Omen" is passive — a thing that exists. Augury assumes the world is sending information, and that attention is the act of receiving it.
usage: He had learned augury young and never entirely stopped practicing it.

definition: Golden in color or quality; of a gilded light; also: ornate in literary style.
lost: Named the specific register of gold-light, which is warmer and heavier than yellow and more earned than bright.
usage: An aureate afternoon light lay across the city and made even the industrial quarter seem momentarily forgiven.

definition: An omen read from the flight of birds; any patronage or protective guidance.
lost: The modern word collapsed into its metaphor — we kept 'under the auspices of' but lost the bird, and the bird was doing the work.
usage: They set out under good auspice: three herons flying north, and no clouds past the ridge.

definition: Evil, woe, destruction — not as a moral judgment but as a force; the active quality of harm, the thing in things that unmakes. Used in compounds: baleful, balefire.
lost: "Evil" is moral. Bale is elemental — not a judgment but a property, the way cold is a property of winter.
usage: Whatever had passed through here, it had left its bale in the soil.

definition: Bound by a debt of gratitude; owing something to someone in a way that outlasts the original favor.
lost: Named the social debt that doesn't appear on ledgers — beholden is the obligation you carry in how you speak about someone.
usage: She was beholden to no one, which she had arranged deliberately and at some cost.

definition: Perhaps, probably — but with a quality of resigned expectation rather than mere uncertainty. The speaker already suspects the answer.
lost: "Maybe" is open. Belike suggests you already know, and are preparing yourself.
usage: "Belike she's heard about the rest of them," he said, and did not finish the thought.

definition: In good time; early; before it was too late — carrying the implication that the earliness was deliberate, that the choice to act before the hour was a considered one.
lost: "Early" is positional. Betimes carries intention — and sometimes relief that the intention was followed.
usage: They left betimes, while the road was still quiet and the border guards still sleeping.

definition: A favor formally requested; a granted petition; a gift given in response to a specific ask.
lost: We kept boon as 'windfall' but lost the formal structure — a boon was asked, not merely received.
usage: She would grant him one boon, she said, and he should choose it carefully.

definition: A sound, report, or rumor; to spread a report; the sound of gossip traveling.
lost: Held noise and rumor as the same force — bruit understood that information and sound both travel by air.
usage: The bruit of the execution reached the port before the tide turned.

definition: To laugh loudly, harshly, and without restraint; to laugh in a way that fills a room whether welcome or not.
lost: Named the laugh that the laugher cannot control — not joyful, but released.
usage: He cachinnated at the wrong moment and the interview was effectively over.

definition: A false and malicious accusation; deliberate misrepresentation designed to damage a reputation.
lost: Named deliberate dishonesty without using the word 'lie' — calumny is a lie with a specific target and intent.
usage: The calumny had been in circulation for three years before anyone thought to check its source.

definition: Of or relating to dead bodies and the places where they accumulate; having the matter-of-fact quality of a house of bones — not gothic horror but practical mortality.
lost: We have euphemisms. Charnel is the word for when you're past them.
usage: The smell was charnel and old; whatever had died here had been dying for years.

definition: To call or name a thing; to give it its true designation. More formal than "call" — implying the act of naming carries consequence.
lost: "Call" is casual. Clepe implies the name, once given, binds both parties.
usage: By what name do they clepe this road in the old maps?

definition: Pleasing in appearance; well-proportioned; attractive without being exceptional.
lost: Named the register of beauty that doesn't make demands — comely is attractive within a human scale, not beyond it.
usage: She was comely in the way of people who have aged without argument, in good proportion with themselves.

definition: The legal practice of clearing oneself of a charge by oath-helpers who swear to one's innocence.
lost: Named a legal institution built on the social weight of a person's standing — compurgation assumed that who will swear for you is evidence of who you are.
usage: He called for compurgation, and twelve men of the parish stood and swore, and it was enough.

definition: Of the same blood; related by birth; sharing a common ancestor.
lost: Named blood relation as a legal and moral category, not merely a biological fact — consanguinity created obligations.
usage: They were consanguine, which the law treated as a fact and they treated as a complication.

definition: To flash and sparkle; to throw off light in intermittent bursts; to glitter in motion.
lost: Gave motion to light — coruscate is light doing something, not just being somewhere.
usage: The harbor coruscated with the broken reflections of a thousand lanterns.

definition: A solemn, binding agreement; a contract that carries moral or spiritual weight beyond its legal terms.
lost: Named the agreement that means more than its clauses — a covenant is not voided by a technicality because it was never merely technical.
usage: Their arrangement was not a contract; it was a covenant, and that distinction meant everything when it was tested.

definition: A dry crackling or rattling sound — the sound of things that have lost their moisture, their flexibility; the sound of brittle things under pressure, or of fire consuming paper.
lost: "Crackling" is onomatopoeic but imprecise. Crepitation specifies the quality — dry, rapid, repetitive, the sound of material yielding.
usage: The crepitation of the burning papers was the only sound in the room for a long time.

definition: The brief, specific moment of twilight — neither day nor night, the threshold state. A temporal condition of radical uncertainty.
lost: "Dusk" is the result. Crepuscule is the event, with all its instability intact.
usage: He always made his approach at crepuscule, when shapes were still uncertain.

definition: To grow dark; to become obscure; to lie hidden in the dark.
lost: We have no verb for darkness increasing — 'darkle' gave the process agency that 'getting darker' refuses.
usage: The coast darkling behind them, they rowed toward the open sea.

definition: An object that caused a person's death, legally forfeit to the crown as an offering to God.
lost: Held objects morally accountable in a way modern law refuses — a deodand is a thing that owes a debt.
usage: The coroner declared the millwheel a deodand; it was sold and the price given to the church.

definition: A binding judgment; a legal decision; fate as something decreed rather than merely arriving.
lost: We kept doom but stripped its judicial precision — Old English doom was a verdict, not a catastrophe.
usage: The doom was read aloud in the square so that none could claim ignorance of it.

definition: Radiant; pouring forth light; shining brilliantly outward.
lost: Distinguished light as emanation from light as reflection — effulgent things produce light, they do not merely catch it.
usage: She walked into the room effulgent with a certainty that had no obvious cause.

definition: Shortly afterward; again, and soon — carrying the sense of inevitable return, of history's tendency to repeat with only cosmetic variation.
lost: "Soon" looks forward only. Eftsoons carries a memory of the last time.
usage: The river rose, and eftsoons the lower fields were lost again.

definition: A formal speech or piece of writing in praise of a person or thing.
lost: Gave praise a structure and a cost — an encomium was a performance you prepared, not a comment you left.
usage: The encomium ran to forty minutes and the subject of it sat looking increasingly uncomfortable.

definition: To invest with a fief or estate; to bind someone to land and service by a formal grant.
lost: Named the act of binding land to people — enfeoffment created obligations that ran with the soil, not just with the individual.
usage: He enfeoffed his youngest son with the eastern pastures and the obligations that ran with them.

definition: The condition of wandering without fixed purpose; the state of being one who travels by necessity of nature rather than plan — a recognized mode of being, with its own traditions and codes.
lost: "Wandering" is a gerund. Errantry is a vocation — a recognized state that carries its own dignity.
usage: Errantry had taught her more than any settled life could offer, but the cost was considerable.

definition: At a former time; belonging to the past — carrying the specific quality of something that was genuinely present and substantial and is now absent; a former state that was real.
lost: "Former" is administrative. Erstwhile implies the loss of the thing has changed the landscape around it.
usage: Her erstwhile certainty had been replaced by something more functional but less comforting.

definition: Willing, content to, eager — but carrying a quality of resigned willingness; the acceptance of what must be done, stated as preference because preference and necessity have converged.
lost: "Willing" is neutral. Fain implies the willingness has been thought through, perhaps reluctantly.
usage: "I am fain to let it go," she said, and she meant it, though it had cost her considerable effort to mean it.

definition: Sworn loyalty to a lord or cause; the formal acknowledgment of a bond of obligation.
lost: Named loyalty as a witnessed, sworn act — fealty is loyalty that has been made public and therefore costs something.
usage: She had sworn fealty once, and she intended to be the kind of person for whom once was sufficient.

definition: Fated to die; having otherworldly second sight; marked by knowledge that comes from beyond the living world.
lost: We kept fey but stripped its prophetic weight — it now means whimsical, which is the exact opposite of what it once named.
usage: There was something fey about the way she described the flood three days before it came.

definition: Utterly ruined or exhausted; done completely, past all recovery — not merely tired but finished; the state that follows the expenditure of the very last reserve.
lost: "Exhausted" is a condition. Fordone is a conclusion — the cost has been totaled and found to exceed what was available.
usage: They found him fordone at the boundary stone, unable to account for the preceding three days.

definition: In truth; indeed — used to introduce a statement offered as simple fact, with the minor force of insisting that what follows is not debatable, not embellished, merely so.
lost: "Indeed" is affirmation. Forsooth is a small declaration — what follows is offered as truth without apology.
usage: "Forsooth, he was there before any of us arrived," said the innkeeper, and was believed.

definition: Utterly exhausted; spent completely to the very end of one's capacity, with nothing in reserve and no expectation of quick recovery.
lost: "Exhausted" is clinical. Forspent implies a reckoning — the full cost has been paid.
usage: She arrived forspent, and slept for three days without once speaking.

definition: The crash of something breaking; the loud noise of a thing coming apart.
lost: Named the sound of destruction specifically — fragor is what you hear when something that was whole is no longer.
usage: The fragor of the chandelier brought everyone from the adjoining rooms.

definition: Brilliantly, dazzlingly shining; radiant with an intensity that demands acknowledgment.
lost: Distinguished intensity from mere presence — fulgent light is light that makes a claim on you.
usage: The fulgent face of the glacier turned every shadow blue.

definition: Of or pertaining to soot and smoke; dark, sooty; possessing the quality not of fire but of what fire leaves behind — residue, aftermath, the evidence of burning.
lost: "Smoky" describes the process. Fuliginous describes what remains after the process is complete.
usage: The ceiling was fuliginous with generations of candle-smoke and kept secrets well.

definition: Pompous, inflated, empty language; writing that mistakes weight for substance.
lost: Named a specific failure of prose — fustian is language that has dressed up without having anything to say.
usage: The report was pure fustian: twelve pages of impressive vocabulary covering an absence of conclusion.

definition: A wanderer, vagabond; one who cannot remain; a companion on the road.
lost: Named aimless travel as a character type, not a temporary condition — a gadling is constitutionally incapable of staying.
usage: She was a gadling by birth; every place she settled became a departure point within the year.

definition: To deny, contradict, or speak against something that has been declared — specifically to refuse what has been publicly stated, to stand against it with words.
lost: "Contradict" is clinical. Gainsay implies a public act — a refusal of what has been put forward, with all the consequence that entails.
usage: None who had seen it could gainsay the account, though it required believing difficult things.

definition: A faint or guttering light; a candle or lantern barely holding against the dark. The word suggests not the source of light but its quality of imminent failure.
lost: "Candlelight" describes the source. Glim describes the quality of its failure.
usage: She navigated the passage by glim alone, the flame bending with each step.

definition: The period of twilight after sunset — not the moment of dusk but the slow interval that follows, when daylight has ended but darkness has not yet committed to anything.
lost: "Dusk" is the moment. Gloaming is the atmosphere — the quality of light that makes shapes uncertain and distances unreliable.
usage: By gloaming the roads were empty and the distances had become impossible to judge.

definition: Direct spiritual knowledge received rather than learned; knowing as revelation, not study.
lost: Named the epistemological category that education cannot produce — the thing you suddenly know without having been taught.
usage: It was not something she could explain; it arrived as gnosis, complete and uninvited.

definition: A reward given for service rendered; recompense of a formal or ceremonial kind.
lost: Named reward as something earned and witnessed, not merely given — a guerdon is a public acknowledgment.
usage: He asked no guerdon for the crossing except the name of the man he carried.

definition: Divination by the examination of animal entrails — specifically the liver; more broadly, any practice of reading the interior of things for knowledge that the surface does not offer.
lost: We kept the metaphor — "gut feeling" — and lost the word for the practice of taking it seriously.
usage: She had no patience for haruspicy but could not argue with the results.

definition: Mental dullness; bluntness of feeling and perception; the state of having been worn smooth.
lost: Named dullness as a condition rather than a trait — hebetude is something that happens to you, not something you are.
usage: The months of paperwork had produced in him a thorough hebetude that no amount of sleep could shift.

definition: Before this time; up until the present moment — a word that draws a precise line between what existed in all the time before now, and the now that has changed it.
lost: "Previously" is neutral. Heretofore implies the present moment is a rupture — there was a before, and you are in the after.
usage: Nothing heretofore had prepared him for the specific quality of that silence.

definition: An untranslatable longing for a home — or a version of yourself — that no longer exists and perhaps never did exist quite as remembered.
lost: "Nostalgia" is medical in origin and implies a cure. Hiraeth implies none.
usage: The hiraeth came not for places but for the person she had been in them.

definition: A formal, public act of allegiance; the ceremony by which a vassal acknowledged a lord's authority.
lost: Named the body's performance of loyalty — homage was not internal but enacted, witnessed, recorded.
usage: He paid homage without irony, which surprised everyone who thought they knew him.

definition: A spoken curse; the act of calling down evil or harm through deliberate utterance.
lost: Distinguished the curse as a performative speech act from the harm it invokes — an imprecation is a thing you do with your mouth.
usage: She left without violence, but the imprecation she murmured at the threshold was heard by three people.

definition: Of the color of raw flesh; blood-red; to make red as blood.
lost: Held flesh and wound in the same word — incarnadine is the color of both beginnings and ends.
usage: The water was incarnadine by the time they reached the shore, and they understood what that meant.

definition: Embodied in flesh; existing in physical form; an abstraction given a body.
lost: Named the condition of having a body as a specific theological and philosophical state — to be incarnate is to have crossed a threshold.
usage: Standing there in the rain, she seemed to be grief incarnate, stripped of everything incidental.

definition: Traveling from place to place without fixed habitation; defined by motion rather than destination.
lost: Named the condition of permanent transit — an itinerant is not between places but has made transit itself the address.
usage: He had been itinerant so long that the question of where he lived produced a small, rehearsed hesitation.

definition: One's acquaintances, neighbors, and familiar people; one's home country and its people.
lost: We kept kith only in the phrase kith and kin, which no one says separately — kith alone named the looser circle of belonging, the people who know your name without being family.
usage: He had left his kith so long ago that returning felt less like homecoming than research.

definition: Softly bright; playing lightly over a surface without burning; light that moves rather than settles.
lost: Named a quality of light that 'glowing' misses — lambent light is alive, it moves across things rather than emanating from them.
usage: The lambent firelight played across the maps spread on the table.

definition: A pleasant or oppressive physical heaviness; the body slowed to a tempo just below waking.
lost: Named the ambiguous state where exhaustion and pleasure blur — languor is tiredness that might be enjoyable, and that ambiguity mattered.
usage: A summer languor settled over the house from noon until the sea wind came back at five.

definition: Physical or mental weariness; exhaustion that goes deeper than sleep can reach.
lost: Distinguished exhaustion from tiredness — lassitude is not solved by rest, which is why it requires its own word.
usage: A lassitude had settled into her bones that she recognized as something other than lack of sleep.

definition: Gladly, willingly; with equal willingness — used in constructions like 'I had as lief' meaning 'I would just as gladly.' A way of stating preference as equivalence.
lost: Modern English has no clean equivalent for this construction. To say you would 'just as soon' is weaker; 'I had as lief die' cannot be translated without losing something.
usage: "I had as lief stay," he said, which was true, though no one believed him.

definition: One's rightful lord or sovereign; also: bound by feudal loyalty; owing or owed allegiance.
lost: Held both sides of the bond in one word — a liege was simultaneously the one owed loyalty and the one who owed protection.
usage: My liege, he said, and meant it; he had not served the man forty years in order to hedge the word now.

definition: Lithe and supple; bending easily; having the physical grace of something with no unnecessary stiffness.
lost: Named flexibility as a bodily quality, not merely a metaphor — lissom is about what a body can do without effort.
usage: She moved with a lissom ease through the rigging that made the new hands stare.

definition: Skill or cunning in the use and arrangement of words; verbal craft; the quality of a writer or speaker who shapes language with the same precision a carpenter gives to wood.
lost: "Wordsmith" is a metaphor of manufacture. Logodaedaly names the specific skill — the cunning, the architecture, the knowing use of the instrument.
usage: His logodaedaly was such that you only noticed, afterward, that you had been led.

definition: Glowing with soft light; translucent; emitting a quiet, interior luminescence.
lost: Glowing replaced it but dropped the translucency — lucent things glow from inside.
usage: The stone was lucent in moonlight, as though it had spent the day storing warmth.

definition: To free from slavery; to release from bondage by formal legal act.
lost: Named liberation as a specific legal performance — to manumit was to undo a legal act with another legal act, which made the freedom real in the same register as the captivity.
usage: He manumitted his last remaining bond-servant on the day the law made the gesture redundant.

definition: Low spirits; depression without spectacular cause; a heaviness that settles in without event.
lost: Named the condition of being simply, dully low — megrims has no drama, which is precisely the point.
usage: He could not explain the megrims to anyone; it seemed too small a thing to name and too large to carry.

definition: Thick, impenetrable darkness; a blackness that has weight and texture.
lost: Modern English lost a word for darkness that felt physical — dark tells you about light, mirk tells you about what you're walking through.
usage: The mirk of the close swallowed him before he reached the second door.

definition: In the process of dying; in terminal decline; no longer capable of development.
lost: Applied to institutions as well as bodies — moribund named the long slow end that dying doesn't quite capture.
usage: The tradition was moribund before anyone admitted it; they kept celebrating it out of a shared reluctance to say so.

definition: Wandering throughout the world; one who travels not toward anything but continuously, treating the earth itself as the itinerary rather than the route.
lost: "World traveler" implies a home to return to. Mundivagant implies the traveling is the home.
usage: The mundivagant gave no fixed address and required none.

definition: A continuous low murmuring sound; also: a flock of starlings in collective, wheeling flight.
lost: Named two things that share a quality — the flock and the sound both move in patterns too complex to track individually.
usage: The murmuration turned above the estuary as the light failed, and no one said anything.

definition: Soft, tender; weak or vulnerable to cold; having flesh that feels too much.
lost: Named the specific vulnerability of a body not hardened to its climate — nesh has no modern equivalent because we stopped valuing the distinction.
usage: She was nesh in a way that made northern winters a sustained personal insult.

definition: Shining in or by night; specifically of the high, faint clouds that catch the sun after it has set for everyone below and glow alone against full dark.
lost: We have no common word for this. The phenomenon kept its name only in the scientific register, where it lost all strangeness.
usage: The noctilucent clouds were the only witnesses to what happened on the ridge.

definition: Wandering by night; given to nocturnal roaming.
lost: Named a practice that streetlights and risk aversion have nearly extinguished — the night had its own kind of traveller.
usage: She was by nature noctivagant, more alive at midnight than at noon.

definition: A record or journal kept specifically of things observed, thought, or done during the night-hours. A chronicle of what the day refuses to hold.
lost: A diary is for days. Noctuary acknowledges that the night requires its own accounting.
usage: What the noctuary contained, he never shared with morning company.

definition: Filled with a sense of the divine or supernatural; having a quality of sacred awe that borders on dread. The feeling of standing in the presence of something vast.
lost: "Spiritual" is comfortable. Numinous retains the original terror of the encounter.
usage: The clearing was numinous in a way that discouraged investigation.

definition: Of or relating to dreams; having the quality of a dream — not merely surreal but specifically possessing the logic, the certainty, the categorical shifts that belong only to the dreaming mind.
lost: "Dream-like" is a comparison. Oneiric is a quality — a condition of the object itself, independent of any observer's state.
usage: The whole morning had an oneiric quality; she checked, twice, that she was actually awake.

definition: To harden into bone; to become rigid and incapable of change; to lose flexibility permanently.
lost: Applied to institutions and minds as well as bodies — ossify names a process that is both biological and moral.
usage: The policy had ossified over decades until no one could remember why it had been written.

definition: A vessel or chamber for the bones of the dead — not a tomb, which houses the body; an ossuary is what remains after the body has been superseded by the skeleton, the last record of a life.
lost: "Burial place" is a destination. Ossuary names the function — the keeping and containing of what remains after everything else has left.
usage: The ossuary was small and very old, predating the church that had eventually been built around it.

definition: In or to some other place; elsewhere — but carrying a suggestion of unreachability, of a distance that is not merely physical.
lost: "Elsewhere" is a direction. Otherwhere is a state of permanent displacement.
usage: Her thoughts were otherwhere long before her body followed.

definition: An unnatural paleness; the specific whiteness of a face when something is wrong.
lost: Named paleness as a diagnostic — pallor is not a complexion but a signal, and the distinction is what makes it useful.
usage: The pallor of his hands when he set the letter down told her everything before he spoke.

definition: A sudden, sharp pain — physical or emotional; a quick, piercing sensation that arrives without warning.
lost: Named the quality of sudden-onset pain: its brevity and its completeness — a pang is over almost before you register it.
usage: A pang of something — guilt, or its near neighbor — crossed his face before he looked away.

definition: To travel, especially on foot over a long distance; to journey through foreign places.
lost: Named long walking as a purposeful act — to peregrinate is to travel as a practice, not just a displacement.
usage: He had peregrinated through four countries before settling, briefly, in a village that forgot him almost immediately.

definition: Light, teasing, deflective talk; banter designed to avoid engagement while appearing sociable.
lost: Named the conversational mode that looks like wit but is actually evasion — persiflage is the opposite of rede.
usage: She met every serious question with persiflage so fluid that you only realized the deflection after she'd left the room.

definition: A bitter, sustained verbal attack; a speech of denunciation.
lost: Named the register of formal verbal assault — a philippic is not an outburst but a prepared instrument.
usage: He delivered his philippic calmly, which made it considerably worse to receive.

definition: The full moon; or the period of its fullness — understood not as a calendar phase but as a condition the world enters, a state of maximum illumination before the slow diminishing begins.
lost: "Full moon" is a phase label. Plenilune names the experience of standing under it.
usage: At plenilune, the courtyard was bright enough to read by, and nobody slept.

definition: To pledge solemnly; to bind oneself by promise; a solemn engagement.
lost: Named the act of pledging as distinct from the pledge — to plight your troth was to perform a binding, not merely to feel one.
usage: He had plighted himself to her before witnesses, and he was not the kind of man to regard that as a formality.

definition: An omen or portent; to foretell; a feeling of what is about to occur.
lost: Gave foreboding a dignified name — presage is an omen that deserves to be heeded, not merely a feeling.
usage: The silence of the birds was presage enough; he turned back before the path bent out of sight.

definition: The sound of wind moving through leaves and branches — specifically the quality of that sound, which is both plural and continuous, many things making one indistinct whispering.
lost: We have no common word for this. The sound exists everywhere; its name was lost and with it the recognition that the experience deserved one.
usage: The psithurism was constant enough that it had taken on the quality of a voice just below comprehension.

definition: Physical beauty, especially of a striking or exceptional kind.
lost: Gave beauty a blunt, almost clinical weight — pulchritude is beauty stated as a fact, not admired from a distance.
usage: His pulchritude was the kind that photographs never quite caught, which meant it was entirely in the movement.

definition: To give life to; the moment a fetus first moves in the womb; to become alive.
lost: Named the specific threshold between not-yet-living and living — quicken is the moment, not the state, and that distinction is irreplaceable.
usage: She felt it quicken on a Tuesday, and afterward the whole pregnancy was different.

definition: A discharge from a debt or obligation; a receipt that closes the account.
lost: Named the specific moment when something is fully paid and therefore done — quittance is the sound of a ledger closing.
usage: She demanded quittance in writing before she would leave the room.

definition: Counsel, advice — particularly advice given by one who has seen or suffered more, whose knowledge comes not from study but from consequence. Also: a riddle; to interpret.
lost: "Advice" is offered casually. Rede implies the advisor has looked at something directly and shares what they found there.
usage: She had given her rede plainly and without softening, and he had not thanked her for it.

definition: Payment in kind; the return of a deed, whether good or ill, in equivalent measure.
lost: Held both revenge and gratitude as the same structural act — requital is balance restored, not emotion expressed.
usage: He waited years for requital; when it came, it was more quiet than he had imagined.

definition: Black, especially in heraldry; a deep, formal black that implies mourning or gravity.
lost: Gave black a register — sable black is serious, heraldic, carried; it is not merely the absence of color.
usage: He dressed in sable from collar to heel, and no one asked why.

definition: Blood-red in color; also: optimistic, confident, of a hopeful temperament — from the belief that blood was the humor of cheerfulness.
lost: Held the color and the character in the same word because they were once believed to share a cause — the loss of that connection drained both meanings.
usage: He was sanguine about the delay in a way that suggested he knew something the rest of them didn't.

definition: A spark; the smallest possible trace or fragment of something.
lost: Named the unit of light that makes fire conceivable — a scintilla is light at the threshold of existence.
usage: There was not a scintilla of warmth in his reply, and everyone present understood.

definition: To see visions in a reflective or dark surface — water, glass, a mirror turned face-down; to seek knowledge through a medium that offers images without explanation.
lost: "Prophesy" implies certainty about the future. Scry names the method — looking into something dark until it shows you something true, with no guarantee of interpretation.
usage: She could scry but had long since stopped; the images were reliable but the meanings were not.

definition: Everlasting, eternal — but with emphasis on temporal continuity rather than spatial extension; what has always been and will continue being, regardless of what else changes.
lost: "Eternal" is spatial — it goes on. Sempiternal emphasizes the temporal — it precedes you and will outlast you.
usage: The sempiternal indifference of the mountain was, in its own way, a comfort.

definition: A period of seven nights; a week — but measured by darkness rather than days. Time as endurance rather than administration.
lost: "Week" is administrative. Sennight implies the weight of seven sleeps endured.
usage: He had not eaten in a sennight, and the forest showed no signs of ending.

definition: A hissing sound; the production of hissing consonants; the sound of air through narrow spaces.
lost: Gave the hiss a name of its own — sibilation is a sonic object, not just a description.
usage: The sibilation of the gas jet was the only sound in the corridor.

definition: Oracular, mysteriously prophetic; of or resembling a sibyl.
lost: Encoded the specific register of feminine prophecy — not wild, but precise and deeply inconvenient.
usage: Her answer was sibylline enough that everyone in the room found a different warning in it.

definition: One who wanders alone; specifically, one who prefers the solitary road and seeks it out rather than merely tolerating it.
lost: "Loner" carries pathology. Solivagant carries intention.
usage: A confirmed solivagant, she kept no fixed address and required none.

definition: Divination by the casting or drawing of lots; by extension, any practice that uses chance as a vehicle for revelation — the faith that randomness, properly attended to, is not random.
lost: "Divination" is the category. Sortilege names the specific belief — that chance does not obscure the truth but reveals it.
usage: She distrusted sortilege on principle, though the lots had never once been wrong.

definition: A soft moaning or sighing sound — specifically the noise the wind makes moving through things: trees, wire, the gaps in old structures. Not howling, not murmuring, but the exact register between them.
lost: "Howl" is too strong; "murmur" is too gentle. Sough names the specific register — wind in things, at the edge of meaning.
usage: The sough of the rigging was company of a kind, if not conversation.

definition: Irritable and bad-tempered from melancholy; sharp-tongued as a symptom of inner depletion.
lost: Named the specific personality produced by too much black bile — splenetic is not merely grumpy but precisely unwell in a way that makes you cruel.
usage: He grew splenetic by late afternoon each day, which the staff had learned to interpret as hunger.

definition: To wander aimlessly, without destination or the need of one — specifically implying that the aimlessness is preferred, not lamented; a chosen vacancy of purpose.
lost: "Wander" suggests purposelessness. Stravage suggests the purposelessness is the point.
usage: He had stravaged the moors since boyhood and saw no reason to develop a destination.

definition: Of or belonging to the Styx; utterly, inescapably dark; of the underworld.
lost: Gave darkness a proper address — not just the absence of light but a specific geography of hopelessness.
usage: The stygian water of the flooded mine swallowed their torchlight completely.

definition: A pledge or guarantee; one who answers for another's obligations; the person who stakes themselves on someone else's word.
lost: Named the guarantor as a distinct and serious role — a surety puts their own standing at risk on behalf of another person's promise.
usage: He offered himself as surety for the debt, which was either very confident or very foolish.

definition: A continuous whispering or murmuring sound; the act of murmuring.
lost: Sister to susurrus but a process, not a state — susurration is the ongoing act, the duration of the sound.
usage: A susurration ran through the congregation before the doors opened.

definition: A whisper, murmur, or continuous soft sound — the quality of voices or water or machinery at the edge of hearing; sound that exists at the threshold between information and atmosphere.
lost: "Whisper" requires a speaker. Susurrus names the quality of the sound itself, independent of its origin.
usage: There was a susurrus from the walls that she had learned not to investigate.

definition: A dream or vision — particularly one that arrives with weight, that carries the quality of communication, as if something were attempting to reach you through sleep.
lost: "Dream" is neutral. Sweven implies the dream was sent rather than merely happening.
usage: The sweven returned three nights running, always ending at the same locked door.

definition: To faint; a sudden loss of consciousness; the body's surrender to an overload it cannot process upright.
lost: Named the body's retreat with more dignity than 'faint' allows — a swoon implies you were feeling something worth losing consciousness over.
usage: She did not swoon at the news; she went very still, which was more alarming.

definition: Profound, sacred shadow; the darkness of Holy Week; shadows as a liturgical event.
lost: Named darkness as something attended and witnessed rather than merely suffered.
usage: She sat in the tenebrae of the nave long after the candles had been extinguished one by one.

definition: Dark, shadowed, obscure — specifically a darkness that has qualities, texture, weight; a darkness that feels inhabited rather than merely unlit.
lost: "Dark" is a condition. Tenebrous implies the darkness is doing something.
usage: The passage grew tenebrous long before the torches had any reason to fail.

definition: Muscular strength; the visible quality of bodily power; sinew and vigor as a physical presence.
lost: Gave strength a texture — thew is not an abstract capacity but something you can see in the shoulders of someone who has it.
usage: He had the thew of someone who had carried things for a living, and it showed in the way he stood.

definition: Bondage; a person held in bondage; captivity that is complete and not merely temporary.
lost: Named slavery with the weight of capture — a thrall is not merely unfree but held, which is a different and heavier thing.
usage: In thrall to an opinion he could not quite defend, he defended it anyway.

definition: The sound of bells ringing; the continuous, overlapping music of struck metal.
lost: Named a sonic texture that 'ringing' flattens — tintinnabulation is bells as an environment, not an event.
usage: The tintinnabulation from the cathedral filled the square every quarter-hour until it became part of how silence felt.

definition: Sluggish, dormant, numbed to sensation; the state of a creature in winter suspension.
lost: Named the specific quality of hibernation-in-humans — torpid is not tired, it is the system slowing to minimum.
usage: He spent the winter months torpid, emerging only when the cold required it.

definition: From beyond the mountains; a stranger or foreigner; also: a cold north wind from over the Alps.
lost: Named the stranger by their origin rather than their strangeness — a tramontane is defined by what they crossed, not what they lack.
usage: They regarded him as tramontane: arriving from somewhere hard, knowing things the valley didn't.

definition: Pledged faith, sworn loyalty — the word used when loyalty is not merely held but formally given, bound by the act of speaking it. To plight one's troth is to transfer something irretrievable.
lost: "Loyalty" is a virtue you hold. Troth is a commitment you make — the binding is in the speaking.
usage: She had given her troth in the old form, and considered herself held by it regardless of what had followed.

definition: To believe, suppose, or trust in a thing — with a quality of quiet, considered conviction. Not a guess but a held conclusion.
lost: "Think" is provisional. Trow implies the belief has been weighed and kept.
usage: "He'll not be coming back, I trow," said the innkeeper, and refilled the glass.

definition: A sustained wail or howl; a rising, modulated cry that carries intensity itself rather than any specific emotion — the vocal form of extremity, equally available to grief and ecstasy.
lost: "Wailing" implies grief. Ululation names the sound — the intensity that precedes and outlasts whatever specific feeling produced it.
usage: The ululation rose from somewhere in the lower city and did not stop until dawn.

definition: Of or relating to shadow; dwelling in shadow; the darkest part of an eclipse.
lost: Gave shade a technical precision — the umbra is the zone where no light survives at all.
usage: An umbral calm settled over the garden as the eclipse reached its full depth.

definition: Staying in the shade; reclusive; averse to open roads and exposure.
lost: Named the counter-wanderer — the person who stays is also a type, and umbratic gave them precision.
usage: His umbratic disposition made him excellent at correspondence and useless on any kind of expedition.

definition: The cry of a newborn; the first sound made at the opening of a life.
lost: Named the cry that begins everything — the only sound with no precedent in the life that produces it.
usage: A vagitus from the back room told him the ordeal was over.

definition: To prophesy; to speak as an oracle; to foretell with authority.
lost: Named the act of prophecy as a specific verbal performance, not just a lucky guess.
usage: The old man would vaticinate from the steps each morning, and the fishermen had long since stopped laughing.

definition: Green; fresh; alive with the particular green of growing things.
lost: Gave green a specific register — virid is not any green but the green of things that are growing because they have no choice.
usage: The hillside was virid in a way that felt aggressive after months of grey.

definition: Pale, faint, depleted of color and vitality; the appearance of something that has given what it had. Not merely pale but specifically the pallor of depletion.
lost: "Pale" describes appearance. Wan describes depletion — the color has gone because something else went with it.
usage: He returned wan and said almost nothing for the first week.

definition: Traveling, especially on foot; the state of being one who travels roads. Used as a noun, it describes a condition of life rather than a single act of transit.
lost: "Traveling" is logistical. Wayfaring is an identity — a way of being in the world.
usage: The wayfaring had aged him less than the years spent standing still.

definition: Wellbeing; prosperity; the common good understood as a thing held collectively.
lost: Named collective prosperity as something with defenders — the common weal was a shared possession, not a policy outcome.
usage: He had served the weal his whole career and was surprised to find it had not returned the favour.

definition: The sky, the vault of heaven — but understood as a dome with weight and shape, not merely the absence of ground. The welkin has a ceiling; the sky does not.
lost: "Sky" is descriptive. Welkin carries the sense of a vault — something arched above you with intent.
usage: The birds rose until the welkin swallowed them whole.

definition: Blood-price; the monetary compensation owed to a family for the killing of one of their kin.
lost: Encoded the radical idea that a life has a calculable social value — not sentimental, but precise and public.
usage: The wergild was paid in silver over three years, and after the third payment, the feud was formally ended.

definition: Attractive and appealing; charming in a way that draws people toward rather than merely impressing them.
lost: Named beauty that welcomes — winsome is the opposite of beauty that intimidates, and that distinction no longer has a word.
usage: There was something winsome about his confusion that made everyone in the room want to help him.

definition: In addition to all this; nevertheless; at the same time — a hinge word that holds contradictions in a single breath without resolving them.
lost: "But" forces a choice. Withal holds the tension open and lets it stand.
usage: She was exhausted and, withal, unwilling to stop while daylight remained.

definition: Fate as an active, weaving force; destiny not as destination but as process.
lost: Fate implies a fixed end point — wyrd implied something woven continuously, which is a far more unsettling idea.
usage: He had stopped fighting his wyrd by the time the messenger arrived.

definition: Hospitable to strangers; welcoming of the unknown guest.
lost: Named a virtue the modern world has largely stopped aspiring to — xenodochial hospitality assumes the stranger deserves welcome before they've earned it.
usage: The village had a xenodochial tradition older than its church: the road through meant the gate was open.
